title: Calculating NRR for a Bootstrapped SaaS Under $50k MRR
meta_description: How to calculate net revenue retention for a sub-$50k MRR SaaS without fooling yourself. A founder's messy, honest walkthrough with real numbers.
h1: I Calculated NRR Wrong for Eight Months. Here's What Actually Works Under $50k MRR.
body_markdown:
In March 2023 I told an angel investor my NRR was 118%. He asked how I calculated it. I froze, because the honest answer was "I subtracted churn from expansion and divided by something, I think." He didn't invest. Looking back, he was right not to — my number was garbage, and so was my understanding of what net revenue retention actually measures for a bootstrapped SaaS at our stage.

This post is the version of the NRR conversation I wish someone had given me when we were at $8k MRR and trying to sound like adults on calls.

## What NRR actually measures (and why the SaaStr definition will mislead you at $20k MRR)

Net revenue retention is the percentage of recurring revenue you keep from an existing cohort of customers over a period, including expansion, contraction, and churn — but excluding new logos.

The formula everyone quotes:

**NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion − Contraction − Churn) / Starting MRR**

Simple. Except the definitions of "starting," "expansion," and "churn" get weird fast when you're small.

Here's the thing nobody tells you: the public NRR numbers you see from Snowflake (158% in their 2021 S-1), Datadog (~130% consistently), or Monday.com aren't comparable to yours. Those are calculated on dollar-weighted cohorts with hundreds of thousands of customers, where a few enterprise upsells smooth out the entire curve. When you have 47 customers, one person upgrading from the $49 plan to the $199 plan spikes your NRR by a full percentage point. One churn drops it by two.

So the first honest thing to say is: at under $50k MRR, NRR is a noisy signal. Useful, but noisy. Treat it like weather, not a thermostat.

### The cohort trap I fell into

My original mistake was calculating NRR on "all customers active on the first of the month." Sounds reasonable. It's wrong.

The problem: if a customer signed up on January 28th and churned February 5th, they count as a loss in my February NRR even though they barely existed as a retained customer. At small scale, those edge cases dominate. I was reporting NRR swings of 15 percentage points month to month and reading meaning into noise.

The fix — which I got from a 2019 ChartMogul blog post that I still reference — is to calculate NRR on customers who were active at the **start of the period and had been customers for at least one full billing cycle**. You're measuring retention, not trial-to-paid conversion. Those are different things.

## The calculation, with real numbers from a 2-person SaaS

Let me walk through an actual month. These are lightly adjusted from a founder I talked to in October — call him M. He runs a project management tool, two-person team, $31k MRR at the time.

On September 1, he had 284 customers generating $29,400 MRR. Of those 284:

- 11 churned during September (lost $1,180 MRR)
- 6 downgraded plans (lost $310 MRR)
- 19 upgraded or added seats (gained $890 MRR)
- 248 stayed exactly the same

He also signed 23 new customers in September worth $2,100 MRR. **Those don't count for NRR.** That's critical. NRR only tracks the September 1 cohort.

So:

NRR = ($29,400 + $890 − $310 − $1,180) / $29,400 = **95.2%**

Gross revenue retention (same formula minus the expansion):

GRR = ($29,400 − $310 − $1,180) / $29,400 = **94.9%**

His NRR was barely above his GRR. That's the real story. He'd been telling people he was at "108% NRR" based on a previous month where three annual plans happened to renew with seat expansions. One good month isn't a trend. His honest trailing-three-month NRR was 97%.

And 97% is fine for a bootstrapped SaaS under $50k MRR. I want to say that clearly because the VC-blog-industrial-complex has convinced founders that anything under 110% is a failing business. It isn't.

## Why your NRR target is not 120%

The 120%+ NRR benchmark comes from public SaaS companies selling to mid-market and enterprise, where expansion happens through seat growth as customer companies grow. If you sell a $29/month tool to solopreneurs and freelancers, your customers literally cannot expand much. A freelancer isn't going to 5x their seat count.

Jason Cohen wrote a thing back in 2015 (I think it was on his blog, not the WP Engine one) about how SMB SaaS has structurally higher churn and lower expansion than enterprise, and you should benchmark yourself against your segment, not against Zoom. He was right then and nobody listened, and founders are still beating themselves up about it a decade later.

Realistic NRR bands for bootstrapped SaaS at your stage, based on what I've seen across about 30 founders in a Slack group I'm in:

1. **Pure SMB/prosumer, <$99/mo plans**: 85-95% NRR is normal. Above 100% is great.
2. **SMB with usage-based or seat-based pricing**: 95-105% is the realistic band.
3. **Mid-market, $500+/mo plans**: 100-115% is achievable and what you should aim for.
4. **Enterprise-lite with annual contracts**: 110%+ is reasonable but unstable at small scale.

If you're doing $12k MRR selling a $19/month Chrome extension, stop comparing yourself to Gong.

## The specific ways I was fooling myself

I want to be direct about this because I burned eight months on it.

First, I was counting annual plan renewals as "expansion" when the customer simply paid the same amount again. That inflated NRR by about 6 points. An annual renewal at the same price is neither expansion nor contraction — it's retention. Flat.

Second, I was using Stripe's "MRR" number from the dashboard, which includes one-time charges and weird proration artifacts. If you've ever upgraded a customer mid-cycle, you know Stripe's MRR can spike for a day and then settle. Calculating NRR off daily Stripe snapshots is — I'll say it — insane. You need normalized monthly recurring revenue where $120/year plans count as $10/month, not $120 in the month they renewed.

Third — and this is the one that stung — I wasn't tracking contraction separately from churn. A customer downgrading from $99 to $29 is not the same as a customer leaving. But I'd been lumping them together as "revenue lost" and it made my retention story incoherent. When I separated them, I realized 40% of my "churn" was actually downgrades, which meant my product wasn't failing to deliver value — it was mispriced for a segment of customers who wanted a lighter tier. Totally different problem, totally different fix.

> The number that matters isn't your NRR. It's whether you understand what's driving it.

## How to actually track this without losing your weekends

The brute force approach: export Stripe data to a Google Sheet, tag each customer with their September 1 MRR, then reconcile against their September 30 MRR. Takes about two hours the first time, 30 minutes monthly once you have the template.

I did this for a year. It worked. It also made me hate the first Monday of every month.

The dedicated tool approach: Baremetrics ($129/mo for the Startup plan as of late 2024), ChartMogul (free up to $10k MRR, then paid), ProfitWell (free but acquired by Paddle in 2022 and increasingly Paddle-flavored). All three will calculate NRR for you. All three also calculate it slightly differently, which is its own rabbit hole — Baremetrics and ChartMogul disagreed by about 4 points on the same Stripe data when I tested them in 2023.

If you want the number without the monthly Baremetrics bill, which felt steep when we were at $14k MRR, [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) pulls your Stripe data and gives you NRR, GRR, and a clean cohort view without the dashboard spelunking. It's what I built after getting tired of maintaining the spreadsheet. Not a magic answer — the math is still the math — but it saves the two hours.

The thing I'd tell 2022-me is that the tool doesn't matter nearly as much as the discipline of calculating the same number the same way every month for twelve months. One year of consistent, slightly-wrong NRR is more useful than twelve months of different methodologies that each feel right at the time. Pick a definition. Write it down. Stick with it. Revisit in a year.

At $8k MRR you don't need perfect NRR reporting. You need to know whether customers are sticking around and spending more, less, or the same. That's three numbers, tracked monthly, on a line chart. Everything else is decoration — and honestly, at our scale, most of the decoration is what keeps us from looking at the actual business.
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, minimal concept art style. A stylized line chart made of thin neon cyan strokes floating against a deep navy background, where the chart line fractures into smaller splintered shards as it extends to the right, suggesting noise and volatility at small scale. Abstract geometric cohort shapes — circles of varying sizes — orbit the chart, some fading, some growing. Subtle grid texture. Muted magenta accent on one shard. Premium tech magazine aesthetic, think Stripe Press or Increment Magazine. No text, no people, no stock imagery.
